channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: NASA launched an emergency mission to stop the Swift Observatory from crashing to Earth - The Verge
item.description:
1. NASA launched an emergency mission to stop the Swift Observatory from crashing to Earth  The Verge
  2. Daring rescue mission launches to save a 3,200-pound NASA observatory from an untimely end  CNN
  3. Rescue mission launches to save NASA telescope that's falling back to Earth thanks to solar storms  PBS
  4. Pegasus launches Swift reboost mission  SpaceNews
  5. A Rescue Mission to Save NASA’s Swift Telescope Launches to Orbit  The New York Times

item.title: The Rubin Observatory's 10-year movie of the universe is about to 'blow our minds,' chief scientist says - Live Science
item.description:
1. The Rubin Observatory's 10-year movie of the universe is about to 'blow our minds,' chief scientist says  Live Science
  2. ‘The greatest cosmic movie ever made’: Historic telescope kicks off an unprecedented survey  CNN
  3. World's largest digital camera starts mapping the universe  Euronews
  4. Rubin Observatory begins 10-year sky survey with stunning image  Mashable
  5. Ocean of Stars  Rubin Observatory

item.title: Astronomers discover Earth’s new ‘next-door neighbor’ — a potentially habitable planet - New York Post
item.description:
1. Astronomers discover Earth’s new ‘next-door neighbor’ — a potentially habitable planet  New York Post
  2. Astronomers discover a potentially habitable planet just 25 light-years away. 'This one is exciting'  Space
  3. UC Irvine astronomers discover a new Earth-like exoplanet  UC Irvine News
  4. A world just 25 light-years away sits in the narrow zone where liquid water could exist on its surface — and astronomers at UC Irvine have now revised its mass down to just 2.3 times Earth’s, making it look less like a gas-shrouded mini-Neptune and more like s  Space Daily
  5. Potentially Habitable Super-Earth Found Just 25 Light-Years Away  ScienceAlert

item.title: Here’s How This ‘Hobbit’-Like Human Ancestor Survived on an Island with Komodo Dragons - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Here’s How This ‘Hobbit’-Like Human Ancestor Survived on an Island with Komodo Dragons  Gizmodo
  2. Hobbit-like human relatives may have been on a less advanced evolutionary path  CNN
  3. ‘Hobbit’ hominins scavenged meat left over by Komodo dragons  New Scientist
  4. Our ancient ‘hobbit’ cousins ate dragon leftovers  National Geographic
  5. New Theory of Smallest Human: Not a Hunter, But Eater of Lizard Leftovers  Haaretz

item.title: The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery  Space Daily
  2. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 on a four-year mission, and in November 2026, nearly half a century later, it will become the first object ever to reach one light-day from Earth — so distant that a signal now takes a full 24 hours to cross the emptiness back home  19FortyFive
  3. Voyager 1 isn't dying because anything broke — after almost 50 years it's simply running out of power, and NASA is shutting off its instruments one by one until the signal goes silent.  19FortyFive

item.title: NASA's Chandra telescope reveals Milky Way's outer reaches may stretch farther than previously known - Fox News
item.description:
1. NASA's Chandra telescope reveals Milky Way's outer reaches may stretch farther than previously known  Fox News
  2. XMM-Newton helps revise distance to outer spiral arms  European Space Agency
  3. NASA’s Chandra Examines Milky Way at Arms’ Length  NASA Science (.gov)
  4. The Milky Way’s arms might not look as we thought  EarthSky
  5. Milky Way arms are further out in space than previously known, NASA says  Yahoo

item.title: NASA says it will isolate volunteers from the outside world for a year - The Register
item.description:
1. NASA says it will isolate volunteers from the outside world for a year  The Register
  2. NASA Seeks Volunteers for Yearlong Simulated Mission to Moon, Mars  NASA (.gov)
  3. NASA needs volunteers to spend a year locked in a Mars simulation  Scientific American
  4. NASA is looking for volunteers to live in a space simulator for a year  Yahoo
  5. NASA Needs Volunteers For Simulated Off-World Missions  Orbital Today

item.title: Live coverage: Semiconductor manufacturing test bed to fly alongside Starlink satellites on Falcon 9 launch - Spaceflight Now
item.description:
1. Live coverage: Semiconductor manufacturing test bed to fly alongside Starlink satellites on Falcon 9 launch  Spaceflight Now
  2. SpaceX Fourth of July weekend launch from Florida. When is liftoff?  Florida Today
  3. SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket launches 24 Starlink satellites from California (video)  Space
  4. SpaceX rocket launch tomorrow from Cape Canaveral: Everything to know  Yahoo
  5. SpaceX Completes Successful Falcon 9 Mission With Starlink Payload  Orbital Today

item.title: A University of Washington researcher published a finding in 2025 that stops you cold: blue whales sing at frequencies so deep that killer whales, their only natural predator, cannot hear them at all — their calls are invisible to orca ears beyond one kilometre - Space Daily
item.description: A University of Washington researcher published a finding in 2025 that stops you cold: blue whales sing at frequencies so deep that killer whales, their only natural predator, cannot hear them at all — their calls are invisible to orca ears beyond one kilometre  Space Daily

item.title: Cooling a data centre on Earth can consume 10 to 30 per cent of its energy budget and millions of litres of water; in orbit, there is no air, no cooling towers, and no river to dump heat into — only vast radiator panels bleeding waste heat away as infrared light int - Space Daily
item.description: Cooling a data centre on Earth can consume 10 to 30 per cent of its energy budget and millions of litres of water; in orbit, there is no air, no cooling towers, and no river to dump heat into — only vast radiator panels bleeding waste heat away as infrared light int  Space Daily

item.title: Physicists Simulated a Black Hole in a Lab. Then It Started to 'Evaporate'. - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Physicists Simulated a Black Hole in a Lab. Then It Started to 'Evaporate'.  ScienceAlert
  2. Backreaction of stimulated Hawking radiation in an optical analogue  Nature
  3. Hawking radiation breakthrough offers new insight into black hole physics  Innovation News Network
  4. Scientists Make Major Black Hole Discovery That Could Explain Stephen Hawking's Greatest Mystery  NDTV
  5. Scientists observe key Hawking radiation process in black hole laboratory experiment  madhyamamonline.com

item.title: For much of the past century, scientists believed dinosaurs were already in decline when the asteroid struck 66 million years ago — but a 2025 study published in Science found fossils in New Mexico showing they were flourishing in diverse, thriving ecosyste - Space Daily
item.description: For much of the past century, scientists believed dinosaurs were already in decline when the asteroid struck 66 million years ago — but a 2025 study published in Science found fossils in New Mexico showing they were flourishing in diverse, thriving ecosyste  Space Daily

item.title: A Chinese Spacecraft Just Arrived At Earth’s ‘Quasi Moon’ - Forbes
item.description:
1. A Chinese Spacecraft Just Arrived At Earth’s ‘Quasi Moon’  Forbes
  2. China’s Tianwen-2 mission has (probably) arrived at a quasi-moon of Earth  The Planetary Society
  3. A tiny “quasi-moon” called Kamoʻoalewa shadows Earth’s orbit and may be a piece of the actual Moon blasted into space by an ancient impact — and China’s Tianwen-2 spacecraft is now closing in to bring a sample back to Earth.  Space Daily
  4. China's Tianwen-2 Probe Has Likely Reached Earth's "Quasi-moon" Kamo‘oalewa. Now The Fun Begins  IFLScience
  5. “Tianwen-2” Lands on Kamoʻoalewa. What Is This Mission and What Is Its Purpose?  Universe Space Tech

item.title: Greenland meltwater adds to AMOC weakening, but updated model finds no tipping point in sight - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Greenland meltwater adds to AMOC weakening, but updated model finds no tipping point in sight  Phys.org
  2. An unusual ‘cold blob’ in the Atlantic is a warning sign for our future weather, researchers say  The Boston Globe
  3. As the Ocean Warms, a 'Cold Blob' in the Atlantic Has Puzzled Scientists. It Might Be a Warning Sign About a Key Current System  Smithsonian Magazine
  4. A Cold Atlantic Anomaly Returns as Super El Niño Shapes Winter 2026/2027 Early Forecast Signals  Severe Weather Europe
  5. Atlantic Cold Blob. A Weak Point in Warming?  Holistic News

item.title: Two enormous structures — the Giant Arc and the Big Ring — appear in the same region of deep space at the same cosmic distance, a pairing so unexpected that Alexia Lopez, one of the astronomers who discovered them, says current cosmology struggles to - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Two enormous structures — the Giant Arc and the Big Ring — appear in the same region of deep space at the same cosmic distance, a pairing so unexpected that Alexia Lopez, one of the astronomers who discovered them, says current cosmology struggles to  Space Daily
  2. Mammoth Structure Found in Deep Space Challenges Our Understanding of The Universe  ScienceAlert
  3. A Massive Ring-Like Structure of Galaxies Defies the Universe's Rules — Here's Why It's Such a Mystery  Discover Magazine
  4. ATLANTIC SKIES: Why the discovery of two 'cosmic superstructures' is challenging a long-held scientific belief  PNI Atlantic News

item.title: America at 500: Where will we be in space in 2276? - Space
item.description:
1. America at 500: Where will we be in space in 2276?  Space
  2. America 250: From 1776 to the moon and beyond (A Space.com series)  Space
  3. Nation marks 250th with record flyovers, art and global tributes  MSN
  4. NASA’s Role In Space Exploration: Rivals – But No Equal  NASA Watch
  5. America 250: How grit and ingenuity fueled the race to the moon  FOX 13 Tampa Bay

item.title: Smithsonian Starstruck VR exhibit lets you stroll through the stars - Ars Technica
item.description:
1. Smithsonian Starstruck VR exhibit lets you stroll through the stars  Ars Technica

  3. Smithsonian Starstruck (Fever), at Level 1, 206 Bourke Street, Melbourne – virtual reality review  theblurb.com.au
  4. Smithsonian Starstruck: an immersive VR experience opens at Orlando’s Fever Hub  Attractions Magazine
  5. First look: 'Smithsonian Starstruck' launches in San Antonio  San Antonio Express-News

item.title: Titan, Saturn’s Largest Moon, Could Become a ‘Gas Station’ for Deep Space Missions - ZME Science
item.description:
1. Titan, Saturn’s Largest Moon, Could Become a ‘Gas Station’ for Deep Space Missions  ZME Science
  2. Could humans someday explore Saturn's moon Titan, or will humanoid robots do it for us?  Space
  3. Titan’s Resources and their Utilization  Astrobiology Web
  4. ⛽ Titan, the interplanetary gas station  Techno-Science.net
  5. Methane, natural fuel lakes, ice, and a nitrogen-rich atmosphere make Titan central to NASA's plans for the first "space fuel station" to speed up missions beyond Mars.  CPG Click Oil and Gas

item.title: Science news this week: James Webb telescope finds a never-before-seen substance, China's 'Great Green Wall' grows faster than natural trees, and a Medici murder mystery is solved - Live Science
item.description: Science news this week: James Webb telescope finds a never-before-seen substance, China's 'Great Green Wall' grows faster than natural trees, and a Medici murder mystery is solved  Live ScienceSee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: A genetic analysis of nearly 700,000 people identified 74 distinct locations in the human genome tied to anxiety, 39 of which had never been linked to the condition before - Space Daily
item.description: A genetic analysis of nearly 700,000 people identified 74 distinct locations in the human genome tied to anxiety, 39 of which had never been linked to the condition before  Space Daily

item.title: What did the night sky look like on the 1st Independence Day 250 years ago? - Space
item.description:
1. What did the night sky look like on the 1st Independence Day 250 years ago?  Space
  2. The Sky Our Founders Saw  thedailypost.digital
  3. Space science has come a long way since July 4, 1776. Here's a look back at the saga  Yahoo
  4. Colorado Skies: Founding Fathers changed human history under stars much like today’s  Reporter-Herald
  5. How viewing the heavens has changed since 1776 | Sky Shorts  Canton Repository

item.title: Why Antarctica froze millions of years before the Arctic – new research - The Conversation
item.description:
1. Why Antarctica froze millions of years before the Arctic – new research  The Conversation
  2. Here's why the South Pole froze over before the North Pole  Reuters
  3. The Breakup of Gondwana Over 100 Million Years Ago May Be Why Antarctica Has Ice Today  eos.org
  4. Why did the Antarctic ice sheet grow many million years earlier than the Arctic ice?  GFZ
  5. Scientists discover why South Pole froze over before the North Pole | Antarctica was once part of Gondwana | Inshorts  Inshorts

item.title: Human flight was still 7 years away in 1776. Now, we're headed back to the moon - Space
item.description:
1. Human flight was still 7 years away in 1776. Now, we're headed back to the moon  Space
  2. NASA’s Artemis II Breaks Agency Streaming Record  NASA (.gov)
  3. Moonbound memories  Wahpeton Daily News
  4. The record Apollo 13 set for the farthest humans had ever travelled from Earth was never meant to be a record — it was a survival manoeuvre after an explosion, and Artemis II quietly surpassed it on a clear April morning in 2026  Space Daily
  5. Take Me to the Moon: A Virtual Evening with NASA  Maine Public

item.title: Salt clouds discovered on iconic 'Pink Planet' as scientists finally get a good look at frosty orb - New York Post
item.description:
1. Salt clouds discovered on iconic 'Pink Planet' as scientists finally get a good look at frosty orb  New York Post
  2. ‘Pink Planet’ surrounded by salty clouds: study  NewsNation
  3. Salty Clouds aren’t the only strange thing about this object in space : Short Wave  NPR
  4. NASA uncovers decade-old mystery surrounding the 'pink planet'  Báo VietNamNet
  5. The Pink Planet has a salty secret  Popular Science

item.title: The debate over how many Earth-like planets exist has a wide range — from one, if the Rare Earth hypothesis is right, to something approaching a hundred quintillion, if Milky Way rates hold across the universe's roughly two trillion galaxies — and both answer - Space Daily
item.description: The debate over how many Earth-like planets exist has a wide range — from one, if the Rare Earth hypothesis is right, to something approaching a hundred quintillion, if Milky Way rates hold across the universe's roughly two trillion galaxies — and both answer  Space Daily

item.title: A martian rock has lots of carbon on it, and it’s not clear why - Ars Technica
item.description:
1. A martian rock has lots of carbon on it, and it’s not clear why  Ars Technica
  2. NASA’s Curiosity rover has identified the most diverse set of organic molecules yet found on Mars, including seven never before detected there, from a sample drilled in 2020 and studied for years before the chemistry finally came into focus  Space Daily
  3. Ancient Martian River Channel Yields Complex Organics  Universe Today
  4. Discovery on Mars: NASA detects possible signs of microbial life  Diario AS
  5. Guest column: Did Earth life reach Mars?  pottsmerc.com

item.title: This tropical creature lives almost 3 times longer than expected and appears to barely age. Here’s why scientists are so excited - BBC Wildlife Magazine
item.description:
1. This tropical creature lives almost 3 times longer than expected and appears to barely age. Here’s why scientists are so excited  BBC Wildlife Magazine
  2. Scientists found tropical butterflies that can live nearly a year and show little sign of ageing; here’s why they may matter for longevity research  The Times of India
  3. Could the secrets of longevity be revealed by a butterfly?  BioTechniques
  4. Most butterflies die in weeks, but these tropical species may be cracking the code of aging  Yahoo
  5. A Slowly Aging Butterfly Could Help Scientists Understand Longevity  The Debrief

item.title: We Asked Pest Experts If Diatomaceous Earth Really Kills Ticks, and They All Said the Same Thing - The Spruce
item.description: We Asked Pest Experts If Diatomaceous Earth Really Kills Ticks, and They All Said the Same Thing  The Spruce

item.title: Mammals use the same underlying system—preserved through evolution—to process smells - Phys.org
item.description: Mammals use the same underlying system—preserved through evolution—to process smells  Phys.orgSee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: Saturn used to be listed with 83 moons. Then astronomers looked harder, found dozens more, and by 2026 the count had exploded to 285 — so many that Saturn now has nearly three times as many confirmed moons as Jupiter. - Space Daily
item.description: Saturn used to be listed with 83 moons. Then astronomers looked harder, found dozens more, and by 2026 the count had exploded to 285 — so many that Saturn now has nearly three times as many confirmed moons as Jupiter.  Space Daily

item.title: Scientists have found a billion-year-old hidden magma system on Mars that supported life on the Red plane - The Times of India
item.description:
1. Scientists have found a billion-year-old hidden magma system on Mars that supported life on the Red plane  The Times of India
  2. Mars may have once been filled with seas of magma that made the Red Planet habitable  Space
  3. Seismic evidence for a melt-depleted lower crust and transcrustal magmatism on Mars  Nature
  4. Mars ran Earth-like magma systems for billions of years without plate tectonics  Martin Cid Magazine
  5. Mars was once covered by magma seas that could have made the Red Planet habitable  NEWS.am TECH

item.title: Saturn’s rings may be far younger than the planet itself — Cassini-era estimates have placed their age anywhere from about 10 million to a few hundred million years, meaning the rings we see today may not have existed for most of the age of dinosaurs. - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Saturn’s rings may be far younger than the planet itself — Cassini-era estimates have placed their age anywhere from about 10 million to a few hundred million years, meaning the rings we see today may not have existed for most of the age of dinosaurs.  Space Daily
  2. Saturn returns! How to see the ringed planet in July 2026  BBC Sky at Night Magazine

item.title: It's tempting to assume faster engines make faster spacecraft, but Parker Solar Probe's record 430,000 miles per hour came mostly from gravity: seven Venus flybys spent nearly seven years bending its orbit closer to the Sun, until the Sun's own pull accelerate - Space Daily
item.description: It's tempting to assume faster engines make faster spacecraft, but Parker Solar Probe's record 430,000 miles per hour came mostly from gravity: seven Venus flybys spent nearly seven years bending its orbit closer to the Sun, until the Sun's own pull accelerate  Space Daily

item.title: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the lunar surface will remain there, undisturbed, for at least ten million years — there is no wind, no rain, and almost nothing to erase them - Space Daily
item.description: The footprints left by Apollo astronauts on the lunar surface will remain there, undisturbed, for at least ten million years — there is no wind, no rain, and almost nothing to erase them  Space Daily

  4. Rosalind Franklin Mars Rover Gets First Braking Engines: Life-Search Mission Hits 2028 Milestone  Tech Times

item.title: In 1610, Galileo spotted a small, smooth moon circling Jupiter and wrote it down; four hundred years later, scientists confirmed it likely holds the largest ocean in the solar system, buried under ice and never once touched by sunlight - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In 1610, Galileo spotted a small, smooth moon circling Jupiter and wrote it down; four hundred years later, scientists confirmed it likely holds the largest ocean in the solar system, buried under ice and never once touched by sunlight  Space Daily
  2. Radar Echoes From Europa Reveal Secrets Beneath the Ice  Astrobiology Web
  3. Europa’s Ice Shell Secrets Unlocked by Ground Radar Study  Universe Today
  4. Ground-based radar reveals secrets of Europa’s icy shell  Universe Space Tech

1. NASA’s Curiosity Rover is Investigating Unusual Polygon Structures That Look Like a “Giant Martian Honeycomb”  The Debrief

item.title: In 1995, astronomers pointed Hubble at a patch of sky so dark it looked empty and held it there for ten days. The image that came back contained roughly 3,000 galaxies — not stars, but galaxies — many holding billions of stars of their own. - Space Daily
item.description: In 1995, astronomers pointed Hubble at a patch of sky so dark it looked empty and held it there for ten days. The image that came back contained roughly 3,000 galaxies — not stars, but galaxies — many holding billions of stars of their own.  Space Daily

item.title: Mountain lions moved into a tiny Californian preserve. What happened next astounded scientists - BBC Wildlife Magazine
item.description:
1. Mountain lions moved into a tiny Californian preserve. What happened next astounded scientists  BBC Wildlife Magazine
  2. One mountain lion changed the food web in a California suburb, study finds  news - Mongabay
  3. When mountain lions began appearing more often on trail cameras at a small California nature preserve, coyotes and deer shifted away from nighttime activity, and the woody plants those deer used to eat increased sixty four fold  Space Daily
  4. Mountain lions changed everything in this tiny California preserve  ScienceDaily
  5. Mountain lions left this suburb a century ago; one returned to Stanford's preserve in 2015, and woody plan  The Economic Times

item.title: The Sun’s Atmosphere May Be Feeding on Dust - ZME Science
item.description:
1. The Sun’s Atmosphere May Be Feeding on Dust  ZME Science
  2. Cosmic dust could play key role in cracking long-standing mystery of solar corona heating  Phys.org
  3. Why is Sun's atmosphere hotter than its own surface? Long-standing mystery may finally have an answer  starlust.org
  4. Parker Solar Probe Discovers High-Speed Dust Grains Near the Sun, Offering New Insights into Solar Corona's Mysteries  CPG Click Oil and Gas

item.title: Hubble has spotted ‘impossible’ light in deep space, and scientists are trying to explain where it came f - The Times of India
item.description:

  2. Hubble telescope spots 'impossible' light from a galaxy that shouldn't have been visible  Live Science

  4. Hubble Detects Ultraviolet Light from Tiny Starburst Galaxy in Early Universe  Sci.News
  5. 🔭 Hubble sees an object it shouldn't have seen  Techno-Science.net

item.title: In April 2025, four private astronauts on SpaceX’s Fram2 mission became the first humans ever to circle Earth in a true polar orbit, flying directly over both poles — a route no government crewed space program had taken in more than six decades of human sp - Space Daily
item.description: In April 2025, four private astronauts on SpaceX’s Fram2 mission became the first humans ever to circle Earth in a true polar orbit, flying directly over both poles — a route no government crewed space program had taken in more than six decades of human sp  Space Daily

item.title: The word algebra comes from the title of a book called Al-Jabr, written around the year 820 by a Persian mathematician named Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad — and the word algorithm comes from the Latinized versio - Space Daily

item.title: Hunting the tardigrade: one small step in sequencing DNA of all life on Earth - The Guardian
item.description: Hunting the tardigrade: one small step in sequencing DNA of all life on Earth  The Guardian
